local_names.hindi: उबली हुई सफेद चीनी पत्तागोभी (बोक चॉय)

aliases: उबली हुई सफेद चीनी पत्तागोभी (बोक चॉय)

hindi: उबली हुई सफेद चीनी पत्तागोभी (बोक चॉय)

region_of_origin: China; widely cultivated and consumed in Singapore and Southeast Asia

ontology.foodex2_description: Bok choy (Chinese cabbage, pak choi) — boiled
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers Chinese cabbage / pak choi as the closest parent term; boiled preparation facet applied. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for boiled bok choy; closest_parent is the most defensible match in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept identified for boiled bok choy at time of processing.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side serving of boiled bok choy in Singapore is approximately 100 g, equivalent to a small bowl of cooked leaves and stems served alongside rice or noodles.

nutrient_source.title: Boiled white Chinese cabbage (Bok choy) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for boiled white Chinese cabbage (Bok choy), record F14010012. All core macros and micronutrients sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Foster-Powell et al.; analogous estimate for non-starchy leafy vegetables (bok choy / Chinese cabbage)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for boiled bok choy is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 15 based on the analogous non-starchy leafy vegetable category (Chinese cabbage / Brassica family) documented in Foster-Powell et al. (2002) and Atkinson et al. (2008) international GI tables, where non-starchy leafy vegetables consistently register GI < 20. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 1.78) / 100 ≈ 0.27, rounded to 0.3 per 100 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (34 mg/100 g) and high potassium (371 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Boiled bok choy is an excellent DASH-diet vegetable for hypertension management due to its very low sodium content (34 mg/100 g) and high potassium (371 mg/100 g) and calcium (93 mg/100 g); patients should avoid adding oyster sauce or soy sauce, which can substantially increase sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely low carbohydrate (1.78 g/100 g) and very low glycemic index (~15)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Boiled bok choy has negligible glycaemic impact with only 1.78 g carbohydrate and an estimated GI of approximately 15, making it freely suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes; its dietary fibre (1.0 g/100 g) further supports postprandial glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low fat (0.16 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and high potassium with anti-inflammatory cruciferous compounds
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled bok choy is cardioprotective due to its negligible fat (0.16 g/100 g), confirmed zero cholesterol, high potassium (371 mg/100 g), and sulforaphane content from its cruciferous origin, supporting vascular health and lipid profile management; it is suitable for heart-healthy dietary patterns without restriction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory cruciferous compounds (sulforaphane), vitamin C (~26 mg/100 g), and calcium (93 mg/100 g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Boiled bok choy provides sulforaphane, vitamin C, and calcium that support joint health and bone protection in rheumatoid arthritis patients, particularly those on corticosteroids; high-sodium sauces should be avoided as they can worsen RA-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled white Chinese cabbage, commonly known as bok choy or pak choy (Brassica rapa subsp. chinensis), is a leafy green vegetable widely consumed across Singapore, China, and Southeast Asia. The vegetable features crisp white stems and tender dark-green leaves; boiling produces a soft, mildly sweet flavour with a slightly succulent texture. It is typically prepared by blanching or boiling in plain water for 2–4 minutes and served as a side dish alongside rice or noodles, often with a light drizzle of oyster sauce or sesame oil in Singaporean and Chinese cuisine. Nutritionally, boiled bok choy is extremely low in calories (12 kcal/100 g) and carbohydrates (1.78 g/100 g), making it suitable for calorie-restricted, low-carbohydrate, and ketogenic dietary patterns. It provides a meaningful amount of protein for a leafy vegetable (1.56 g/100 g), negligible fat (0.16 g/100 g), and confirmed zero cholesterol. Its micronutrient profile is notable: potassium (371 mg/100 g) supports blood pressure regulation, calcium (93 mg/100 g) contributes to bone health, iron (1.04 mg/100 g) supports haematopoiesis, and vitamin C (~26 mg/100 g) enhances non-haem iron absorption. As a cruciferous vegetable, bok choy contains glucosinolates and sulforaphane, which have documented anti-inflammatory and potential chemoprotective properties. Its very low sodium content (34 mg/100 g) makes it ideal for DASH-diet and hypertension management. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 15 (low), with a glycemic load of approximately 0.3 per 100 g serving, confirming negligible glycaemic impact for Type 2 Diabetes management. Boiled bok choy is vegetarian, vegan, halal, kosher, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: Is bok choy good for diabetes?
How many calories in boiled bok choy?
Is boiled Chinese cabbage good for blood pressure?
Can I eat bok choy on keto?
Is bok choy high in potassium?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (record F14010012) with a confidence of 0.95. This is an exact match to the named food item from the primary regional approved source for Singapore foods. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for non-starchy leafy vegetables, documented with appropriate caveats.
notes: Saturated fat estimated at 0.03 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central reference for boiled bok choy (NDB 11999 / FDC ID 169975), as HPB record does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Singapore record F14010012 for this preparation; USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 169975) was consulted but does not provide all of these fractions for the boiled preparation. Vitamin C value of 26 mg/100 g is carried from the input health_suitability context and is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for boiled bok choy. Cholesterol is confirmed zero per HPB source — this is consistent with the plant origin of bok choy (naturally zero cholesterol). GI of approximately 15 is an analogous estimate for non-starchy leafy vegetables from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. (2008) international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for boiled bok choy was found in the approved GI database. The input occasion_tag 'snack' has been retained in meal_context but supplemented with more appropriate meal contexts for a cooked vegetable side dish. Dietary tag 'keto' from input is supported by the very low carbohydrate content (1.78 g/100 g). FoodEx2 match_type set to 'closest_parent' as A00LC covers Chinese cabbage / pak choi as the nearest parent node; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for the boiled preparation.
